docs.title: Complete Guide: Audit with Docker + LLM
docs.text:
Step-by-step guide to run a comprehensive smart contract security audit using Docker and generate a professional PDF report with AI-powered interpretation.

Estimated setup time: first run ~15 min (image and model downloads). Subsequent runs: only steps 3-5.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text: Requirement Details Docker Desktop Version 20+ with 8GB+ RAM allocated Ollama For AI-powered report interpretation Disk space ~12GB (Docker image ~8GB + LLM model ~4GB)

docs.title: Step 1: Install and configure Docker
docs.text:
Download Docker Desktop from https://www.docker.com/products/docker-desktop

Configure memory (required):

  • Docker Desktop → Settings → Resources → Memory → 8GB (or more)
  • Apply & Restart

Pull the FULL MIESC image (includes all 50 tools):

docker pull ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:full

ARM / Apple Silicon: The :full image in the registry is amd64-only. On ARM it runs under QEMU emulation (~3-5x slower). For native performance, build locally with ./scripts/build-images.sh full or use the setup wizard ./scripts/docker-setup.sh. The :latest (standard) image is multi-arch and works natively on ARM.

Verify it works:

docker run --rm ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:full --version
# Should show: MIESC version 5.1.0

docker run --rm ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:full doctor
# Shows ~30 available tools

docs.title: Step 2: Install Ollama and download the AI model
macOS
docs.text: brew install ollama

docs.title: Linux
docs.text: curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh

docs.title: Windows
docs.text: Download from https://ollama.com/download

docs.title: Start Ollama and download the model
docs.text:
# Start the service (keep this terminal open)
ollama serve

# In another terminal, download the interpretation model (~4GB)
ollama pull mistral:latest

# Verify it's available
ollama list
# Should show: mistral:latest

Note: ollama serve must be running throughout the audit. If you close the terminal, step 5 won't be able to generate AI insights.

docs.title: Step 3: Prepare your contracts
docs.text:
Place all .sol files you want to audit in a folder:

mkdir my_contracts
cp MyToken.sol MyVault.sol my_contracts/

# Verify the files are there
ls my_contracts/
# MyToken.sol  MyVault.sol

docs.title: Step 4: Run the full audit
docs.text:
This runs all 9 defense layers (static, dynamic, symbolic, formal verification, AI, ML, etc.) on every contract in the folder:

docker run --rm \
  -v $(pwd)/my_contracts:/contracts \
  ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:full \
  audit batch /contracts -o /contracts/results.json -p thorough -r

On Windows PowerShell replace $(pwd) with ${PWD}:

docker run --rm `
  -v ${PWD}/my_contracts:/contracts `
  ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:full `
  audit batch /contracts -o /contracts/results.json -p thorough -r

When finished you'll see a summary like this:

    Batch Analysis Summary
╭────────────────────┬────────╮
│ Metric             │  Value │
├────────────────────┼────────┤
│ Contracts Analyzed │      9 │
│ CRITICAL           │      0 │
│ HIGH               │      4 │
│ MEDIUM             │      3 │
│ LOW                │     39 │
│ TOTAL FINDINGS     │     73 │
╰────────────────────┴────────╯
OK Report saved to /contracts/results.json

docs.title: Step 5: Generate the professional PDF report with AI
macOS / Windows
docs.text:
docker run --rm \
  -e OLLAMA_HOST=http://host.docker.internal:11434 \
  -v $(pwd)/my_contracts:/contracts \
  ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:full \
  report /contracts/results.json -t premium -f pdf \
    --llm-interpret \
    --client "Client Name" \
    --auditor "Your Name" \
    --contract-name "Project Name" \
    --network "Ethereum Mainnet" \
    --classification "CONFIDENTIAL" \
    -o /contracts/audit_report.pdf

docs.title: Linux
docs.text:
docker run --rm --network host \
  -e OLLAMA_HOST=http://localhost:11434 \
  -v $(pwd)/my_contracts:/contracts \
  ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:full \
  report /contracts/results.json -t premium -f pdf \
    --llm-interpret \
    --client "Client Name" \
    --auditor "Your Name" \
    --contract-name "Project Name" \
    --network "Ethereum Mainnet" \
    --classification "CONFIDENTIAL" \
    -o /contracts/audit_report.pdf

docs.title: Windows PowerShell
docs.text:
docker run --rm `
  -e OLLAMA_HOST=http://host.docker.internal:11434 `
  -v ${PWD}/my_contracts:/contracts `
  ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:full `
  report /contracts/results.json -t premium -f pdf `
    --llm-interpret `
    --client "Client Name" `
    --auditor "Your Name" `
    --contract-name "Project Name" `
    --network "Ethereum Mainnet" `
    --classification "CONFIDENTIAL" `
    -o /contracts/audit_report.pdf

Customize these fields:

Field What to put Example --client Client or company name "Acme Corp" --auditor Auditor name "Security Team" --contract-name Contract or project name "TokenV2.sol" --network Target deployment network "Ethereum Mainnet", "Polygon", "BSC" --classification Report classification "CONFIDENTIAL", "PUBLIC", "INTERNAL"

docs.title: Step 6: Open the report
docs.text:
The PDF is saved in your contracts folder:

# macOS
open my_contracts/audit_report.pdf

# Linux
xdg-open my_contracts/audit_report.pdf

# Windows
start my_contracts\audit_report.pdf

docs.title: What the report includes
docs.text:
The professional PDF report (Trail of Bits / OpenZeppelin style) includes:

  • Cover page with confidentiality classification
  • Executive summary with model-assisted business risk analysis
  • Deployment recommendation: GO / NO-GO / CONDITIONAL
  • Risk matrix with CVSS-like scoring
  • Detailed findings with step-by-step attack scenarios
  • Code remediation suggestions with diffs
  • Prioritized remediation roadmap
  • PoC exploit templates for critical/high findings
  • AI disclosure note for transparency

docs.title: "Cannot connect to Ollama" or "LLM interpretation failed"
docs.text:
# Verify Ollama is running
ollama list

# If not running, start it
ollama serve

docs.title: "Out of memory" or Docker freezes
docs.text:
• Increase Docker Desktop RAM to 10GB+ in Settings → Resources
  • Close other memory-intensive applications

docs.title: PDF not generated (only HTML output)
docs.text:
PDF conversion requires WeasyPrint. If it fails, the report is saved as HTML. You can convert manually:

# Install weasyprint
pip install weasyprint  # or: brew install weasyprint

# Convert
weasyprint my_contracts/audit_report.html my_contracts/audit_report.pdf

docs.title: "Contract file not found"
docs.text:
# Make sure the volume mount path is correct
# The path INSIDE the container must match the mount point
docker run --rm -v /full/path/to/my_contracts:/contracts \
  ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:full \
  audit batch /contracts -o /contracts/results.json -p thorough -r

docs.title: Quick reference (copy & paste)
docs.text:
# === SETUP (first time only) ===
docker pull ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:full
ollama pull mistral:latest

# === AUDIT (every time) ===
# 1. Make sure Ollama is running
ollama serve &

# 2. Put your .sol files in a folder
mkdir my_contracts && cp *.sol my_contracts/

# 3. Full audit
docker run --rm \
  -v $(pwd)/my_contracts:/contracts \
  ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:full \
  audit batch /contracts -o /contracts/results.json -p thorough -r

# 4. PDF report with AI (macOS/Windows)
docker run --rm \
  -e OLLAMA_HOST=http://host.docker.internal:11434 \
  -v $(pwd)/my_contracts:/contracts \
  ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:full \
  report /contracts/results.json -t premium -f pdf \
    --llm-interpret \
    --client "Client" \
    --auditor "Auditor" \
    -o /contracts/audit_report.pdf

# 5. Open the PDF
open my_contracts/audit_report.pdf

Full documentation: https://fboiero.github.io/MIESC Repository: https://github.com/fboiero/MIESC

docs.title: Guia Completa: Auditoria con Docker + LLM
docs.text:
Guia paso a paso para ejecutar una auditoria completa de smart contracts usando Docker y generar un reporte PDF profesional con interpretacion de IA.

Tiempo estimado de setup: primera vez ~15 min (descarga de imagenes y modelos). Ejecuciones posteriores: solo pasos 3-5.

docs.title: Requisitos
docs.text: Requisito Detalle Docker Desktop Version 20+ con 8GB+ de RAM asignados Ollama Para la interpretacion IA del reporte Espacio en disco ~12GB (imagen Docker ~8GB + modelo LLM ~4GB)

docs.title: Paso 1: Instalar y configurar Docker
docs.text:
Descargar Docker Desktop desde https://www.docker.com/products/docker-desktop

Configurar memoria (obligatorio):

  • Docker Desktop → Settings → Resources → Memory → 8GB (o mas)
  • Apply & Restart

Descargar la imagen FULL de MIESC (incluye las 50 herramientas):

docker pull ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:full

ARM / Apple Silicon: La imagen :full del registry es solo amd64. En ARM corre bajo emulacion QEMU (~3-5x mas lento). Para rendimiento nativo, construi localmente con ./scripts/build-images.sh full o usa el wizard ./scripts/docker-setup.sh. La imagen :latest (standard) es multi-arch y funciona nativamente en ARM.

Verificar que funciona:

docker run --rm ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:full --version
# Debe mostrar: MIESC version 5.1.0

docker run --rm ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:full doctor
# Muestra las ~30 herramientas disponibles

docs.title: Paso 2: Instalar Ollama y descargar el modelo de IA
macOS
docs.text: brew install ollama

docs.title: Linux
docs.text: curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh

docs.title: Windows
docs.text: Descargar desde https://ollama.com/download

docs.title: Iniciar Ollama y descargar el modelo
docs.text:
# Iniciar el servicio (dejarlo corriendo en una terminal)
ollama serve

# En otra terminal, descargar el modelo de interpretacion (~4GB)
ollama pull mistral:latest

# Verificar que esta disponible
ollama list
# Debe mostrar: mistral:latest

Nota: ollama serve debe estar corriendo durante toda la auditoria. Si cerras la terminal, el paso 4 no va a poder generar los insights de IA.

docs.title: Paso 3: Preparar los contratos
docs.text:
Poner todos los archivos .sol que quieras auditar en una carpeta:

mkdir mis_contratos
cp MiToken.sol MiVault.sol mis_contratos/

# Verificar que estan los archivos
ls mis_contratos/
# MiToken.sol  MiVault.sol

docs.title: Paso 4: Ejecutar la auditoria completa
docs.text:
Esto ejecuta las 9 capas de analisis (estatico, dinamico, simbolico, verificacion formal, IA, ML, etc.) sobre todos los contratos de la carpeta:

docker run --rm \
  -v $(pwd)/mis_contratos:/contracts \
  ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:full \
  audit batch /contracts -o /contracts/results.json -p thorough -r

En Windows PowerShell reemplazar $(pwd) por ${PWD}:

docker run --rm `
  -v ${PWD}/mis_contratos:/contracts `
  ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:full `
  audit batch /contracts -o /contracts/results.json -p thorough -r

Al finalizar vas a ver un resumen como este:

    Batch Analysis Summary
╭────────────────────┬────────╮
│ Metric             │  Value │
├────────────────────┼────────┤
│ Contracts Analyzed │      9 │
│ CRITICAL           │      0 │
│ HIGH               │      4 │
│ MEDIUM             │      3 │
│ LOW                │     39 │
│ TOTAL FINDINGS     │     73 │
╰────────────────────┴────────╯
OK Report saved to /contracts/results.json

docs.title: Paso 5: Generar el reporte PDF profesional con IA
macOS / Windows
docs.text:
docker run --rm \
  -e OLLAMA_HOST=http://host.docker.internal:11434 \
  -v $(pwd)/mis_contratos:/contracts \
  ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:full \
  report /contracts/results.json -t premium -f pdf \
    --llm-interpret \
    --client "Nombre del Cliente" \
    --auditor "Tu Nombre" \
    --contract-name "Nombre del Proyecto" \
    --network "Ethereum Mainnet" \
    --classification "CONFIDENTIAL" \
    -o /contracts/reporte_auditoria.pdf

docs.title: Linux
docs.text:
docker run --rm --network host \
  -e OLLAMA_HOST=http://localhost:11434 \
  -v $(pwd)/mis_contratos:/contracts \
  ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:full \
  report /contracts/results.json -t premium -f pdf \
    --llm-interpret \
    --client "Nombre del Cliente" \
    --auditor "Tu Nombre" \
    --contract-name "Nombre del Proyecto" \
    --network "Ethereum Mainnet" \
    --classification "CONFIDENTIAL" \
    -o /contracts/reporte_auditoria.pdf

docs.title: Windows PowerShell
docs.text:
docker run --rm `
  -e OLLAMA_HOST=http://host.docker.internal:11434 `
  -v ${PWD}/mis_contratos:/contracts `
  ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:full `
  report /contracts/results.json -t premium -f pdf `
    --llm-interpret `
    --client "Nombre del Cliente" `
    --auditor "Tu Nombre" `
    --contract-name "Nombre del Proyecto" `
    --network "Ethereum Mainnet" `
    --classification "CONFIDENTIAL" `
    -o /contracts/reporte_auditoria.pdf

Personalizar estos campos:

Campo Que poner Ejemplo --client Nombre del cliente o empresa "Acme Corp" --auditor Nombre del auditor "Fernando Boiero" --contract-name Nombre del contrato o proyecto "TokenV2.sol" --network Red de despliegue "Ethereum Mainnet", "Polygon", "BSC" --classification Clasificacion del reporte "CONFIDENTIAL", "PUBLIC", "INTERNAL"

docs.title: Paso 6: Abrir el reporte
docs.text:
El PDF queda en la carpeta de contratos:

# macOS
open mis_contratos/reporte_auditoria.pdf

# Linux
xdg-open mis_contratos/reporte_auditoria.pdf

# Windows
start mis_contratos\reporte_auditoria.pdf

docs.title: Que incluye el reporte
docs.text:
El reporte PDF profesional (estilo Trail of Bits / OpenZeppelin) incluye:

  • Portada con clasificacion de confidencialidad
  • Resumen ejecutivo con analisis de riesgo de negocio generado por IA
  • Recomendacion de despliegue: GO / NO-GO / CONDICIONAL
  • Matriz de riesgo con scoring CVSS
  • Hallazgos detallados con escenarios de ataque paso a paso
  • Sugerencias de remediacion con diffs de codigo
  • Hoja de ruta de remediacion priorizada
  • PoC de exploits para hallazgos criticos/altos
  • Nota de disclosure IA para transparencia

docs.title: "Cannot connect to Ollama" o "LLM interpretation failed"
docs.text:
# Verificar que Ollama esta corriendo
ollama list

# Si no esta corriendo, iniciarlo
ollama serve

docs.title: "Out of memory" o Docker se cuelga
docs.text:
• Aumentar la RAM de Docker Desktop a 10GB+ en Settings → Resources
  • Cerrar otras aplicaciones pesadas

docs.title: El PDF no se genera (solo genera HTML)
docs.text:
La conversion a PDF necesita WeasyPrint. Si falla, el reporte se guarda como HTML. Se puede convertir manualmente:

# Instalar weasyprint
pip install weasyprint  # o: brew install weasyprint

# Convertir
weasyprint mis_contratos/reporte_auditoria.html mis_contratos/reporte_auditoria.pdf

docs.title: "Contract file not found"
docs.text:
# Verificar que el volumen esta bien montado
# El path DENTRO del container debe coincidir con el mount
docker run --rm -v /ruta/completa/mis_contratos:/contracts \
  ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:full \
  audit batch /contracts -o /contracts/results.json -p thorough -r

docs.title: Referencia rapida (copiar y pegar)
docs.text:
# === SETUP (solo la primera vez) ===
docker pull ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:full
ollama pull mistral:latest

# === AUDITORIA (cada vez) ===
# 1. Asegurate de que Ollama este corriendo
ollama serve &

# 2. Poner los .sol en una carpeta
mkdir mis_contratos && cp *.sol mis_contratos/

# 3. Auditoria completa
docker run --rm \
  -v $(pwd)/mis_contratos:/contracts \
  ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:full \
  audit batch /contracts -o /contracts/results.json -p thorough -r

# 4. Reporte PDF con IA (macOS/Windows)
docker run --rm \
  -e OLLAMA_HOST=http://host.docker.internal:11434 \
  -v $(pwd)/mis_contratos:/contracts \
  ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:full \
  report /contracts/results.json -t premium -f pdf \
    --llm-interpret \
    --client "Cliente" \
    --auditor "Auditor" \
    -o /contracts/reporte_auditoria.pdf

# 5. Abrir el PDF
open mis_contratos/reporte_auditoria.pdf

Documentacion completa: https://fboiero.github.io/MIESC Repositorio: https://github.com/fboiero/MIESC

docs.title: MIESC Docker Quick Start
docs.text:
Quick guide to run security audits using MIESC with Docker. No installation required.

Versión en Español

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: Requirement Details Docker Desktop Version 20+ with 8GB+ RAM Ollama For AI-powered analysis (optional but recommended) Disk space ~12GB (Docker image ~4GB + LLM models ~8GB)

docs.title: Install Ollama (for AI features)
docs.text:
# macOS
brew install ollama

# Linux
curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh

# Download AI models (one-time, ~8GB total)
ollama pull deepseek-coder:6.7b
ollama pull codellama
ollama pull mistral

# Start Ollama (keep running in background)
ollama serve

docs.title: 1. Prepare Your Contracts
docs.text:
Create a folder and place your .sol files there:

mkdir contracts
cd contracts

# Copy your contracts here
cp /path/to/MyToken.sol .
cp /path/to/MyVault.sol .

Note: This contracts/ folder is where you place the contracts you want to audit. MIESC will analyze all .sol files found here.

docs.title: Sample Contracts for Testing
docs.text:
If you want to test with vulnerable contracts:

# Download real vulnerable contract examples
curl -O https://raw.githubusercontent.com/crytic/not-so-smart-contracts/master/reentrancy/Reentrancy.sol
curl -O https://raw.githubusercontent.com/crytic/not-so-smart-contracts/master/integer_overflow/integer_overflow_1.sol

Repositories with vulnerable contracts for demos:

Repository Description crytic/not-so-smart-contracts Classic vulnerability examples SunWeb3Sec/DeFiVulnLabs Real DeFi vulnerabilities smartbugs/smartbugs Vulnerable contracts dataset

docs.title: 2. Quick Scan (~30 seconds)
docs.text:
Fast triage analysis:

docker run --rm -v $(pwd):/contracts \
  ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:latest \
  scan /contracts/MyContract.sol

docs.title: 3. Full Audit with AI (5-8 min)
docs.text:
Complete 9-layer audit with 30+ tools:

docker run --rm \
  -e OLLAMA_HOST=http://host.docker.internal:11434 \
  -v $(pwd):/contracts \
  ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:full \
  audit full /contracts/MyContract.sol \
  --skip-unavailable \
  -o /contracts/results.json

docs.title: 4. Batch Audit (All Contracts)
docs.text:
Audit all contracts in the folder:

docker run --rm \
  -e OLLAMA_HOST=http://host.docker.internal:11434 \
  -v $(pwd):/contracts \
  ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:full \
  audit batch /contracts \
  -o /contracts/batch_results.json

docs.title: 5. Generate Professional PDF Report (2-3 min)
docs.text:
docker run --rm \
  -e OLLAMA_HOST=http://host.docker.internal:11434 \
  -v $(pwd):/contracts \
  ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:full \
  report /contracts/results.json \
  -t premium -f pdf \
  --llm-interpret \
  --client "Client Name" \
  --auditor "Your Name" \
  --contract-name "Project Name" \
  -o /contracts/audit_report.pdf

# Open the PDF
open audit_report.pdf   # macOS
xdg-open audit_report.pdf   # Linux

docs.title: 6. Check Available Tools
docs.text:
# Without Ollama (static tools only)
docker run --rm ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:full doctor

# With Ollama (includes LLM adapters)
docker run --rm \
  -e OLLAMA_HOST=http://host.docker.internal:11434 \
  ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:full doctor

docs.title: Folder Structure
docs.text:
After running audits, your folder will look like:

contracts/                    ← Your contracts folder
├── MyToken.sol              ← Your .sol contracts
├── MyVault.sol
├── results.json             ← Audit results (generated)
├── batch_results.json       ← Batch results (generated)
└── audit_report.pdf         ← PDF report (generated)

docs.title: Command Reference
docs.text: Command Time Description scan 30s Quick triage analysis audit quick 1min Basic scan audit full 5-8min 9 layers, 30+ tools, AI audit batch varies Multiple contracts report -t premium 2-3min Professional PDF with AI doctor 10s Show available tools

docs.title: Available Docker Images
docs.text: Image Size Contents ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:latest ~3GB Standard: Slither, Aderyn, Solhint, Foundry (~15 tools) ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:full ~4GB Full: + Mythril, Halmos, Semgrep, Wake (~30 tools)

docs.title: "Cannot connect to Ollama"
docs.text:
# Make sure Ollama is running
ollama list

# If not running, start it
ollama serve

docs.title: "Out of memory"
docs.text: • Increase Docker Desktop RAM to 10GB+ in Settings → Resources

docs.title: Linux users
docs.text:
Use --network host instead of host.docker.internal:

docker run --rm --network host \
  -e OLLAMA_HOST=http://localhost:11434 \
  -v $(pwd):/contracts \
  ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:full \
  audit full /contracts/MyContract.sol

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text:
• Full guide: DOCKER_AUDIT_GUIDE.md
  • Website: https://fboiero.github.io/MIESC
  • Repository: https://github.com/fboiero/MIESC

docs.title: MIESC Docker - Guía Rápida
docs.text:
Guía rápida para ejecutar auditorías de seguridad usando MIESC con Docker. No requiere instalación.

English Version

docs.title: Requisitos Previos
docs.text: Requisito Detalles Docker Desktop Versión 20+ con 8GB+ RAM Ollama Para análisis con IA (opcional pero recomendado) Espacio en disco ~12GB (imagen Docker ~4GB + modelos LLM ~8GB)

docs.title: Instalar Ollama (para funciones de IA)
docs.text:
# macOS
brew install ollama

# Linux
curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh

# Descargar modelos de IA (una sola vez, ~8GB total)
ollama pull deepseek-coder:6.7b
ollama pull codellama
ollama pull mistral

# Iniciar Ollama (mantener corriendo en segundo plano)
ollama serve

docs.title: 1. Preparar Tus Contratos
docs.text:
Crea una carpeta y coloca tus archivos .sol ahí:

mkdir contracts
cd contracts

# Copia tus contratos aquí
cp /ruta/a/MiToken.sol .
cp /ruta/a/MiVault.sol .

Nota: Esta carpeta contracts/ es donde colocas los contratos que quieres auditar. MIESC analizará todos los archivos .sol que encuentre aquí.

docs.title: Contratos de Ejemplo para Pruebas
docs.text:
Si quieres probar con contratos vulnerables:

# Descargar ejemplos reales de contratos vulnerables
curl -O https://raw.githubusercontent.com/crytic/not-so-smart-contracts/master/reentrancy/Reentrancy.sol
curl -O https://raw.githubusercontent.com/crytic/not-so-smart-contracts/master/integer_overflow/integer_overflow_1.sol

Repositorios con contratos vulnerables para demos:

Repositorio Descripción crytic/not-so-smart-contracts Ejemplos clásicos de vulnerabilidades SunWeb3Sec/DeFiVulnLabs Vulnerabilidades DeFi reales smartbugs/smartbugs Dataset de contratos vulnerables

docs.title: 2. Escaneo Rápido (~30 segundos)
docs.text:
Análisis rápido de triaje:

docker run --rm -v $(pwd):/contracts \
  ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:latest \
  scan /contracts/MiContrato.sol

docs.title: 3. Auditoría Completa con IA (5-8 min)
docs.text:
Auditoría completa de 9 capas con 30+ herramientas:

docker run --rm \
  -e OLLAMA_HOST=http://host.docker.internal:11434 \
  -v $(pwd):/contracts \
  ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:full \
  audit full /contracts/MiContrato.sol \
  --skip-unavailable \
  -o /contracts/resultados.json

docs.title: 4. Auditoría por Lotes (Todos los Contratos)
docs.text:
Auditar todos los contratos en la carpeta:

docker run --rm \
  -e OLLAMA_HOST=http://host.docker.internal:11434 \
  -v $(pwd):/contracts \
  ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:full \
  audit batch /contracts \
  -o /contracts/resultados_batch.json

docs.title: 5. Generar Reporte PDF Profesional (2-3 min)
docs.text:
docker run --rm \
  -e OLLAMA_HOST=http://host.docker.internal:11434 \
  -v $(pwd):/contracts \
  ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:full \
  report /contracts/resultados.json \
  -t premium -f pdf \
  --llm-interpret \
  --client "Nombre del Cliente" \
  --auditor "Tu Nombre" \
  --contract-name "Nombre del Proyecto" \
  -o /contracts/reporte_auditoria.pdf

# Abrir el PDF
open reporte_auditoria.pdf   # macOS
xdg-open reporte_auditoria.pdf   # Linux

docs.title: 6. Ver Herramientas Disponibles
docs.text:
# Sin Ollama (solo herramientas estáticas)
docker run --rm ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:full doctor

# Con Ollama (incluye adaptadores LLM)
docker run --rm \
  -e OLLAMA_HOST=http://host.docker.internal:11434 \
  ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:full doctor

docs.title: Estructura de Carpetas
docs.text:
Después de ejecutar auditorías, tu carpeta se verá así:

contracts/                        ← Tu carpeta de contratos
├── MiToken.sol                  ← Tus contratos .sol
├── MiVault.sol
├── resultados.json              ← Resultados de auditoría (generado)
├── resultados_batch.json        ← Resultados por lotes (generado)
└── reporte_auditoria.pdf        ← Reporte PDF (generado)

docs.title: Referencia de Comandos
docs.text: Comando Tiempo Descripción scan 30s Análisis rápido de triaje audit quick 1min Escaneo básico audit full 5-8min 9 capas, 30+ herramientas, IA audit batch variable Múltiples contratos report -t premium 2-3min PDF profesional con IA doctor 10s Mostrar herramientas disponibles

docs.title: Imágenes Docker Disponibles
docs.text: Imagen Tamaño Contenido ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:latest ~3GB Estándar: Slither, Aderyn, Solhint, Foundry (~15 herramientas) ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:full ~4GB Completa: + Mythril, Halmos, Semgrep, Wake (~30 herramientas)

docs.title: "Cannot connect to Ollama"
docs.text:
# Asegúrate de que Ollama esté corriendo
ollama list

# Si no está corriendo, iniciarlo
ollama serve

docs.title: "Out of memory"
docs.text: • Aumenta la RAM de Docker Desktop a 10GB+ en Settings → Resources

docs.title: Usuarios de Linux
docs.text:
Usa --network host en lugar de host.docker.internal:

docker run --rm --network host \
  -e OLLAMA_HOST=http://localhost:11434 \
  -v $(pwd):/contracts \
  ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:full \
  audit full /contracts/MiContrato.sol

docs.title: Documentación
docs.text:
• Guía completa: DOCKER_AUDIT_GUIDE_ES.md
  • Sitio web: https://fboiero.github.io/MIESC
  • Repositorio: https://github.com/fboiero/MIESC

docs.title: Testing MIESC Docker Images v5.4.2
docs.text:
Versión en Español

Instructions to pull and verify the latest Docker images.

docs.title: 1. Clean old cached images (optional)
docs.text: docker rmi ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:latest ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:full 2>/dev/null

docs.title: 2. Pull and verify
docs.text:
# Standard (~2GB, multi-arch: works natively on x86_64 and ARM)
docker pull ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:latest
docker run --rm ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:latest --version
docker run --rm ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:latest doctor

# Full (~3GB, amd64-only in registry)
docker pull ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:full
docker run --rm ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:full --version
docker run --rm ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:full doctor

Both commands should show MIESC version 5.4.2.

docs.title: 3. Run a test scan
docs.text: docker run --rm -v $(pwd):/contracts ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:latest scan /contracts/MyContract.sol

docs.title: 4. Available tags
docs.text:
Tag Contents miesc:latest / miesc:5.4.2 Standard: Slither, Aderyn, Solhint, Foundry (~15 tools) miesc:full / miesc:5.4.2-full Full: + Mythril, Manticore, Echidna, Halmos, PyTorch (~30 tools)

Note: Ignore any older versions (pre-5.4.2) that may appear in the registry. Always use the tags listed above.

docs.title: 5. ARM / Apple Silicon
docs.text:
The :latest (standard) image is multi-arch and runs natively on ARM.

The :full image in the registry is amd64-only. On ARM it runs under QEMU emulation (~3-5x slower). For native performance, build locally:

git clone https://github.com/fboiero/MIESC.git
cd MIESC
./scripts/build-images.sh full

The build script will detect ARM and ask for confirmation (z3-solver compilation takes ~30-60 min). The resulting image runs at native speed.

Alternatively, use the setup wizard which guides you through all options:

./scripts/docker-setup.sh

docs.title: Probar Imágenes Docker de MIESC v5.4.2
docs.text:
English Version

Instrucciones para descargar y verificar las últimas imágenes Docker.

docs.title: 1. Limpiar imágenes en caché antiguas (opcional)
docs.text: docker rmi ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:latest ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:full 2>/dev/null

docs.title: 2. Descargar y verificar
docs.text:
# Estándar (~2GB, multi-arch: funciona nativamente en x86_64 y ARM)
docker pull ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:latest
docker run --rm ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:latest --version
docker run --rm ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:latest doctor

# Completa (~3GB, solo amd64 en el registry)
docker pull ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:full
docker run --rm ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:full --version
docker run --rm ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:full doctor

Ambos comandos deberían mostrar MIESC versión 5.4.2.

docs.title: 3. Ejecutar un escaneo de prueba
docs.text: docker run --rm -v $(pwd):/contracts ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:latest scan /contracts/MiContrato.sol

docs.title: 4. Tags disponibles
docs.text:
Tag Contenido miesc:latest / miesc:5.4.2 Estándar: Slither, Aderyn, Solhint, Foundry (~15 herramientas) miesc:full / miesc:5.4.2-full Completa: + Mythril, Manticore, Echidna, Halmos, PyTorch (~30 herramientas)

Nota: Ignora cualquier versión anterior (pre-5.4.2) que pueda aparecer en el registry. Siempre usa los tags listados arriba.

docs.title: 5. ARM / Apple Silicon
docs.text:
La imagen :latest (estándar) es multi-arch y corre nativamente en ARM.

La imagen :full en el registry es solo amd64. En ARM corre bajo emulación QEMU (~3-5x más lento). Para rendimiento nativo, construye localmente:

git clone https://github.com/fboiero/MIESC.git
cd MIESC
./scripts/build-images.sh full

El script de build detectará ARM y pedirá confirmación (la compilación de z3-solver toma ~30-60 min). La imagen resultante corre a velocidad nativa.

Alternativamente, usa el asistente de configuración que te guía por todas las opciones:

./scripts/docker-setup.sh

docs.title: Installation Guide
docs.text: MIESC requires Python 3.12+ and external security analysis tools.

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/fboiero/MIESC.git
cd MIESC
pip install -r requirements.txt

docs.title: Tool Installation
docs.text: Install security analysis tools based on your needs:

docs.title: Core Tools (recommended)
docs.text:
# Static analysis
pip install slither-analyzer

# Symbolic execution
pip install mythril

# Verify installation
python scripts/verify_installation.py

docs.title: Complete Installation
docs.text:
# Use automated installer for all 17 tools
python install_tools.py

# Select tools interactively or install all

docs.title: Tool-by-Tool
docs.text:
Layer 1 - Static Analysis: 

pip install slither-analyzer        # Slither
cargo install aderyn                 # Aderyn (requires Rust)
npm install -g solhint               # Solhint

Layer 2 - Dynamic Testing: 

brew install echidna                 # Echidna (macOS)
go install github.com/crytic/medusa@latest  # Medusa
curl -L https://foundry.paradigm.xyz | bash && foundryup  # Foundry

Layer 3 - Symbolic Execution: 

pip install mythril                  # Mythril
pip install manticore[native]        # Manticore
pip install halmos                   # Halmos

Layer 4 - Formal Verification: 

pip install certora-cli              # Certora (requires API key)
# SMTChecker: included in solc >= 0.8.0
pip install eth-wake                 # Wake

Layer 5 - AI Analysis: 

# SmartLLM - local LLM via Ollama
brew install ollama                  # macOS
ollama pull deepseek-coder

# GPTScan (optional, requires OpenAI API key)
pip install gptscan
export OPENAI_API_KEY=your_key_here

docs.title: System Requirements
docs.text:
• Python: 3.12+
  • Node.js: 14+ (for Solhint)
  • Rust: 1.70+ (for Aderyn)
  • Go: 1.19+ (for Medusa)
  • Memory: 4GB minimum, 8GB recommended
  • Disk: 2GB for tools + datasets

docs.title: Platform-Specific
docs.text:
macOS: 

brew install python node rust go

Ubuntu/Debian: 

apt-get update
apt-get install python3 python3-pip nodejs npm rustc cargo golang-go

Docker (alternative): 

docker build -t miesc .
docker run -v $(pwd)/contracts:/contracts ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:latest scan /contracts/MyToken.sol

See Docker Quickstart

docs.title: Verification
docs.text:
# Check which tools are available
python scripts/verify_installation.py

# Run test suite
pytest tests/

# Quick benchmark
python scripts/run_benchmark.py

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
Slither fails to install: 

pip install slither-analyzer --no-cache-dir

Mythril z3-solver errors: 

pip install z3-solver==4.12.2.0

Ollama not found: 

# macOS
brew install ollama
# Linux
curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh

Permission errors: 

# Use virtual environment
python -m venv venv
source venv/bin/activate  # Linux/macOS
# venv\Scripts\activate   # Windows

docs.title: Minimal Installation
docs.text:
If you only need static analysis:

pip install miesc
miesc scan contract.sol  # Quick scan with Slither-first static analysis

Framework gracefully degrades when tools are unavailable (DPGA compliance).

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Read docs/index.md for usage examples
  • See Docker Audit Guide for a reproducible walkthrough
  • Check CONTRIBUTING.md for development setup

docs.title: Support
docs.text:
• Issues: https://github.com/fboiero/MIESC/issues
  • Email: fboiero@frvm.utn.edu.ar

docs.title: Mutation Testing Guide
docs.text: This guide explains how to use mutation testing to validate test quality in MIESC.

docs.title: What is Mutation Testing?
docs.text:
Mutation testing measures the effectiveness of your test suite by introducing small code changes (mutants) and checking if tests detect them.

Key Concepts:

Term Definition Mutant A small code change (e.g., + → -, > → >=) Killed Test suite detected the mutant (test failed) Survived Test suite missed the mutant (tests still pass) Score Percentage of mutants killed

Why Use Mutation Testing?

  • Validates test quality beyond code coverage
  • Identifies weak or missing test assertions
  • Finds dead code that tests don't exercise
  • Improves confidence in the test suite

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
# Install mutmut
pip install mutmut

# Or install with dev dependencies
pip install -e ".[dev]"

# Run mutation testing (quick mode)
make mutate-quick

# View results
make mutate-results

# Generate HTML report
make mutate-html

docs.title: Make Targets
docs.text: Target Description make mutate Full mutation analysis (30-60 min) make mutate-quick Quick test on core modules only make mutate-results Show results summary make mutate-html Generate HTML report make mutate-show Show surviving mutant details make mutate-check CI check (fails if score < 60%) make mutate-clean Clean cache files

docs.title: Full Analysis
docs.text:
# Run all mutations
mutmut run

# View summary
mutmut results

# Example output:
# Legend for output:
# 🎉 Killed mutants: 142
# ⏰ Timeout: 3
# 🤔 Suspicious: 0
# 🙁 Survived: 18
# 🔇 Skipped: 5

docs.title: Targeting Specific Files
docs.text:
# Mutate specific module
mutmut run --paths-to-mutate src/core/

# Mutate single file
mutmut run --paths-to-mutate src/core/finding.py

docs.title: Viewing Mutants
docs.text:
# Show all surviving mutants
mutmut show all

# Show specific mutant by ID
mutmut show 42

# Apply mutant temporarily (for debugging)
mutmut apply 42

# Restore original code
mutmut apply 0

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Mutation testing is configured in pyproject.toml:

[tool.mutmut]
# Paths to mutate
paths_to_mutate = "src/"

# Test runner command
runner = "python -m pytest tests/ -x --no-cov -q"

# Files to exclude
paths_to_exclude = [
    "src/*_tool.py",
    "src/miesc_*.py",
    # ... more exclusions
]

# Timeout multiplier
timeout_multiplier = 2.0

# Disabled mutation types
disable_mutation_types = [
    "string",
    "fstring",
    "annotation",
]

docs.title: Configuration Options
docs.text: Option Description Default paths_to_mutate Source directories to mutate Required runner Test command to run pytest paths_to_exclude Files/dirs to skip None timeout_multiplier Test timeout factor 1.0 disable_mutation_types Skip certain mutation types None

docs.title: Mutation Types
docs.text:
mutmut applies various mutation operators:

Type Example Description Arithmetic + → - Change operators Comparison > → >= Boundary conditions Boolean True → False Logic changes Return return x → return None Return values Keyword break → continue Control flow Number 0 → 1 Constant values

docs.title: Disabled Types in MIESC
docs.text:
We disable some mutation types that often produce equivalent mutants:

  • string: String literal changes rarely affect behavior
  • fstring: F-string mutations often produce valid variants
  • annotation: Type hints don't affect runtime

docs.title: Good Score
docs.text:
• > 80%: Excellent test coverage
  • 60-80%: Good, but room for improvement
  • < 60%: Tests need strengthening

docs.title: Surviving Mutants
docs.text:
Surviving mutants indicate potential test gaps:

# View surviving mutant
mutmut show 42

# Example output:
# --- src/core/finding.py
# +++ src/core/finding.py
# @@ -45,7 +45,7 @@
#  def severity_score(self):
# -    return self.severity * 10
# +    return self.severity * 11

Actions:

  1. Add test that catches the mutation
  2. Mark as equivalent mutant (rare)
  3. Refactor code to be more testable

docs.title: GitHub Actions
docs.text:
- name: Run Mutation Testing
  run: |
    pip install mutmut
    mutmut run --paths-to-mutate src/core/ --CI

- name: Check Mutation Score
  run: make mutate-check

docs.title: Score Threshold
docs.text:
The mutate-check target enforces a 60% minimum score:

make mutate-check
# Mutation score: 75.5% (151/200 mutants killed)
# ✓ Mutation score check passed

docs.title: Speed Up Testing
docs.text:
# Use fewer cores
mutmut run --max-workers 2

# Quick feedback mode
mutmut run --paths-to-mutate src/core/ --CI

# Skip slow tests
mutmut run --runner "pytest tests/unit/ -x --no-cov -q"

docs.title: Incremental Testing
docs.text:
# Resume after interruption
mutmut run  # Continues from last mutant

# Reset and start fresh
rm -rf .mutmut-cache
mutmut run

docs.title: Cache Management
docs.text:
# View cache status
ls -la .mutmut-cache/

# Clear cache
make mutate-clean

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Start Small: Test core modules first
  2. Fix Survivors: Address surviving mutants before adding new code
  3. Maintain Score: Keep mutation score above threshold
  4. Regular Runs: Include in PR review process (optional)
  5. Exclude Generated Code: Don't mutate auto-generated files

docs.title: "No mutations generated"
docs.text:
Check that paths_to_mutate points to valid Python files:

ls -la src/core/*.py

docs.title: Tests timeout
docs.text:
Increase the timeout multiplier:

[tool.mutmut]
timeout_multiplier = 3.0

docs.title: Out of memory
docs.text:
Reduce parallelism:

mutmut run --max-workers 1

docs.title: Cache corruption
docs.text:
Clear and restart:

make mutate-clean
mutmut run

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• mutmut Documentation
  • Mutation Testing Theory
  • MIESC Testing Guide

Last updated: February 2026

docs.title: Plugin Development Guide
docs.text: MIESC supports external detector plugins distributed via PyPI. Researchers and developers can publish their own vulnerability detectors as Python packages that MIESC discovers and loads automatically via entry points.

docs.title: How Plugin Discovery Works
docs.text:
MIESC uses Python's importlib.metadata entry points system:

  1. When miesc audit full or miesc scan runs, MIESC queries the miesc.detectors entry point group
  2. All packages installed in the current environment that register detectors in this group are discovered
  3. Enabled detectors are instantiated and run on the target contract
  4. Findings are merged with built-in tool results

docs.title: 1. Project Structure
docs.text:
miesc-my-detector/
├── pyproject.toml
├── README.md
├── src/
│   └── miesc_my_detector/
│       ├── __init__.py
│       └── detectors.py
└── tests/
    └── test_detector.py

docs.title: 2. Detector Implementation
docs.text:
# src/miesc_my_detector/detectors.py
"""My custom vulnerability detector for MIESC."""

from typing import List, Dict, Any


class MyCustomDetector:
    """Detects custom vulnerability pattern."""

    name = "my-custom-detector"
    version = "1.0.0"
    description = "Detects XYZ vulnerability pattern in Solidity contracts"

    def analyze(self, contract_path: str, timeout: int = 300, **kwargs) -> List[Dict[str, Any]]:
        """Run analysis on a contract.

        Args:
            contract_path: Path to the .sol file
            timeout: Maximum execution time in seconds

        Returns:
            List of finding dicts with keys:
                - type: str (vulnerability type identifier)
                - severity: str (CRITICAL | HIGH | MEDIUM | LOW | INFO)
                - title: str (human-readable title)
                - description: str (detailed explanation)
                - location: dict with file, line, function keys
                - confidence: float (0.0 - 1.0, optional)
        """
        findings = []

        with open(contract_path) as f:
            source = f.read()
            lines = source.split("\n")

        # Your detection logic here
        for i, line in enumerate(lines, 1):
            if "delegatecall" in line and "onlyOwner" not in source:
                findings.append({
                    "type": "unprotected-delegatecall",
                    "severity": "CRITICAL",
                    "title": "Unprotected delegatecall",
                    "description": (
                        "Contract uses delegatecall without access control. "
                        "An attacker could change the implementation to a malicious contract."
                    ),
                    "location": {
                        "file": contract_path,
                        "line": i,
                        "function": "",
                    },
                    "confidence": 0.85,
                })

        return findings

docs.title: 3. pyproject.toml
docs.text:
[build-system]
requires = ["setuptools>=45", "wheel"]
build-backend = "setuptools.build_meta"

[project]
name = "miesc-my-detector"
version = "1.0.0"
description = "Custom vulnerability detector plugin for MIESC"
readme = "README.md"
license = {text = "MIT"}
requires-python = ">=3.9"
authors = [
    {name = "Your Name", email = "you@example.com"},
]
dependencies = [
    "miesc>=5.4.2",
]

[project.entry-points."miesc.detectors"]
my-custom-detector = "miesc_my_detector.detectors:MyCustomDetector"

[tool.setuptools.packages.find]
where = ["src"]

The critical line is [project.entry-points."miesc.detectors"] — this registers your detector class so MIESC discovers it automatically after pip install.

docs.title: 4. Multiple Detectors
docs.text:
Register multiple detectors from one package:

[project.entry-points."miesc.detectors"]
flash-loan-attack = "miesc_defi.detectors:FlashLoanDetector"
oracle-manipulation = "miesc_defi.detectors:OracleDetector"
price-slippage = "miesc_defi.detectors:SlippageDetector"

docs.title: 5. Publish to PyPI
docs.text:
# Build
python -m build

# Upload (requires PyPI account)
python -m twine upload dist/*

Users install with: 

pip install miesc-my-detector

No configuration needed — MIESC discovers the plugin automatically.

docs.title: Managing Plugins
docs.text:
# List installed plugins
miesc plugins list

# Install from PyPI
miesc plugins install my-detector

# Search for plugins
miesc plugins search "defi"

# Disable a plugin (keeps installed but skips execution)
miesc plugins disable my-detector

# Enable a disabled plugin
miesc plugins enable my-detector

# Uninstall
miesc plugins uninstall my-detector

# Create a new plugin scaffold
miesc plugins create my-detector --output ./my-plugin/

docs.title: Local Development (without publishing)
docs.text:
For development and testing, install your plugin in editable mode:

cd miesc-my-detector/
pip install -e .

Or place it in the local plugins directory:

cp -r my_detector/ ~/.miesc/plugins/my_detector/

docs.title: Required Interface
docs.text:
Your detector class MUST have:

Attribute/Method Type Description name str Unique identifier (used in findings as tool: "plugin:name") analyze(contract_path, timeout=300) method Main entry point, returns list of finding dicts

docs.title: Optional Attributes
docs.text: Attribute Type Description version str Detector version description str Human-readable description supported_versions list[str] Solidity versions supported (e.g., ["0.8", "0.7"])

docs.title: Finding Schema
docs.text:
{
    "type": str,           # REQUIRED: vulnerability type ID
    "severity": str,       # REQUIRED: CRITICAL | HIGH | MEDIUM | LOW | INFO
    "title": str,          # REQUIRED: short title
    "description": str,    # REQUIRED: detailed explanation
    "location": {          # RECOMMENDED
        "file": str,
        "line": int,
        "function": str,
    },
    "confidence": float,   # OPTIONAL: 0.0 - 1.0
    "fix": str,            # OPTIONAL: remediation suggestion
    "references": list,    # OPTIONAL: links to docs/exploits
}

docs.title: Compatibility
docs.text:
Plugins can declare MIESC version requirements:

dependencies = [
    "miesc>=5.4.2,<6.0.0",
]

MIESC checks compatibility before installation and warns if versions don't match.

docs.title: Testing Your Plugin
docs.text:
# tests/test_detector.py
from miesc_my_detector.detectors import MyCustomDetector


def test_detects_vulnerability():
    detector = MyCustomDetector()
    findings = detector.analyze("tests/fixtures/vulnerable.sol")
    assert len(findings) > 0
    assert findings[0]["severity"] == "CRITICAL"


def test_no_false_positive():
    detector = MyCustomDetector()
    findings = detector.analyze("tests/fixtures/safe.sol")
    assert len(findings) == 0

Run with: pytest tests/ -v

docs.title: Professional Smart Contract Audit Workflow
docs.text:
MIESC v5.4.2 | Professional Auditor Guide

This guide provides a structured workflow for conducting professional smart contract security audits using MIESC.

docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
1. Overview
  2. Prerequisites
  3. Audit Phases
  4. Command Reference
  5. Interpreting Findings
  6. Managing False Positives
  7. Report Generation
  8. CI/CD Integration
  9. Best Practices
  10. Example Workflows

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
MIESC integrates a configured adapter stack across 9 defense layers to provide comprehensive smart contract analysis:

Layer Focus Key Tools 1 Static Analysis Slither, Aderyn, Solhint, Semgrep, Wake 2 Dynamic Testing Echidna, Medusa, Foundry, Hardhat 3 Symbolic Execution Mythril, Manticore, Halmos 4 Formal Verification Certora, Scribble, Solc-verify 5 Economic Analysis DeFi-specific detectors 6 Dependency Audit npm-audit, pip-audit, cargo-audit 7 LLM-Powered Analysis GPTScan, SmartLLM, GPTLens 8 Specialized Audits Bridge monitor, L2 validator 9 Meta-Analysis Cross-tool correlation, consensus

docs.title: Docker (Recommended)
docs.text:
# Standard image (~15 tools, fast)
docker pull ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:5.4.2

# Full image (~30 tools, comprehensive)
docker pull ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:5.4.2-full

# Verify installation
docker run --rm ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:5.4.2 --version

docs.title: Local Installation
docs.text:
pip install miesc==5.4.2
miesc doctor  # Check tool availability

docs.title: LLM Setup (Optional, for AI-powered reports)
docs.text:
# Start Ollama with required models
ollama pull mistral
ollama pull deepseek-coder

# Set environment variable
export OLLAMA_HOST=http://localhost:11434

docs.title: Phase 1: Triage (5 minutes)
docs.text:
Goal: Quick assessment to identify obvious issues and estimate audit scope.

# Quick triage scan
miesc scan contract.sol

# Or with Docker
docker run --rm -v $(pwd):/contracts ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:5.4.2 \
  scan /contracts/Contract.sol

Output: Summary of critical/high/medium/low findings.

Decision Points: - 0 critical/high: Proceed to full audit - Many critical: Flag for immediate client notification - Complex codebase: Consider layer-by-layer approach

docs.title: Phase 2: Quick Audit (15-30 minutes)
docs.text:
Goal: Fast comprehensive scan using primary tools.

miesc audit quick ./contracts -o results-quick.json

Tools Used: Slither, Aderyn, Solhint, Semgrep

When to Use: - Initial client engagement - PR reviews - CI/CD pipelines - Time-constrained assessments

docs.title: Phase 3: Full Audit (1-4 hours)
docs.text:
Goal: Complete 9-layer analysis with all available tools.

# Full audit with all layers
miesc audit full ./contracts -o results-full.json

# Skip unavailable tools (recommended for Docker)
miesc audit full ./contracts --skip-unavailable -o results-full.json

# With specific timeout per tool
miesc audit full ./contracts --timeout 600 -o results-full.json

When to Use: - Pre-deployment audits - High-value contracts (DeFi, bridges, governance) - Compliance requirements - Client deliverables

docs.title: Phase 4: Layer-Specific Analysis (Variable)
docs.text:
Goal: Deep dive into specific security aspects.

# Single layer analysis (syntax: audit layer LAYER_NUM CONTRACT)
miesc audit layer 1 ./contracts  # Static only
miesc audit layer 3 ./contracts  # Symbolic execution
miesc audit layer 7 ./contracts  # LLM analysis

# Run multiple layers sequentially
miesc audit layer 1 ./contracts -o layer1.json
miesc audit layer 3 ./contracts -o layer3.json
miesc audit layer 7 ./contracts -o layer7.json

Layer Selection Guide:

Concern Recommended Layers Code quality Layer 1 (Static) Logic bugs Layer 2 (Dynamic) + Layer 3 (Symbolic) Formal correctness Layer 4 (Formal Verification) DeFi vulnerabilities Layer 5 (Economic) + Layer 7 (LLM) Supply chain Layer 6 (Dependencies) Cross-chain/L2 Layer 8 (Specialized)

docs.title: Phase 5: Report Generation (15-60 minutes)
docs.text:
Goal: Create professional deliverable for client.

# Professional PDF report with LLM interpretation
miesc report results-full.json \
  -t premium \
  --llm-interpret \
  -f pdf \
  -o audit-report.pdf

# Executive summary (for management)
miesc report results-full.json \
  -t executive \
  -f pdf \
  -o executive-summary.pdf

# Technical report (for developers)
miesc report results-full.json \
  -t technical \
  -f markdown \
  -o technical-findings.md

# SARIF for GitHub Code Scanning
miesc export results-full.json -f sarif -o results.sarif.json

docs.title: Core Commands
docs.text: Command Purpose Typical Duration miesc scan <file> Quick triage scan 1-5 min miesc audit quick <dir> Fast 3-tool audit 15-30 min miesc audit full <dir> Complete 9-layer audit 1-4 hours miesc audit layer N <contract> Specific layer analysis Variable miesc audit smart <dir> AI-guided adaptive audit 30-60 min miesc report <json> Generate report 5-60 min miesc export <json> Convert to SARIF/CSV/HTML Instant

docs.title: Utility Commands
docs.text: Command Purpose miesc doctor Check tool availability miesc detect <dir> Auto-detect framework (Foundry/Hardhat/Truffle) miesc tools list List all available tools miesc benchmark <dir> Track security posture over time miesc watch <dir> Real-time file monitoring

docs.title: Common Options
docs.text: Option Description -o, --output <file> Output file path --skip-unavailable Skip tools that aren't installed --timeout <seconds> Per-tool timeout (default: 300) -q, --quiet Minimal output --ci CI mode (exit 1 on critical/high findings) -t, --template Report template (premium, professional, executive, technical) --llm-interpret Add AI-powered analysis to reports -f, --format Output format (json, pdf, html, markdown, sarif)

docs.title: Severity Levels
docs.text: Level Action Required Typical Issues CRITICAL Immediate fix, block deployment Reentrancy, access control bypass, fund drain HIGH Fix before deployment Integer overflow, front-running, oracle manipulation MEDIUM Should fix, review carefully Gas optimization, centralization risks LOW Consider fixing Code style, minor gas inefficiencies INFO Informational Best practices, suggestions

docs.title: Finding Structure
docs.text:
{
  "tool": "slither",
  "severity": "HIGH",
  "confidence": "HIGH",
  "title": "Reentrancy vulnerability",
  "description": "External call followed by state change",
  "location": {
    "file": "Contract.sol",
    "line": 45,
    "function": "withdraw()"
  },
  "swc_id": "SWC-107",
  "cwe_id": "CWE-841",
  "recommendation": "Use checks-effects-interactions pattern"
}

docs.title: Cross-Tool Validation
docs.text:
MIESC's correlation engine validates findings across tools:

Validation Level Meaning Confirmed Multiple tools report same issue High Confidence Tool-specific high confidence Medium Confidence Single tool, medium confidence Needs Review Potential false positive

docs.title: Built-in Correlation Engine
docs.text:
MIESC automatically reduces false positives through:

  1. Cross-tool validation: Findings reported by multiple tools get higher confidence
  2. Pattern matching: Known false positive patterns are filtered
  3. Context analysis: Guards, require statements, and CEI patterns are recognized

docs.title: Manual Filtering
docs.text:
# Filter by minimum confidence
miesc audit full ./contracts --min-confidence high

# Filter by severity
miesc audit full ./contracts --min-severity medium

docs.title: Suppression Comments
docs.text:
In your Solidity code:

// slither-disable-next-line reentrancy-eth
// miesc-ignore: false-positive-reason
function withdraw() external {
    // ...
}

docs.title: False Positive Report
docs.text:
After audit, document false positives for client:

## False Positives

| Finding | Tool | Reason |
|---------|------|--------|
| Reentrancy in withdraw() | Slither | Protected by ReentrancyGuard |
| Integer overflow | Mythril | Solidity 0.8+ has built-in checks |

docs.title: Available Templates
docs.text: Template Audience Contents premium Clients CVSS scores, risk matrix, attack scenarios, remediation professional Clients Standard audit format with findings and recommendations executive Management High-level summary, risk assessment, business impact technical Developers Detailed findings, code snippets, fix examples compliance Compliance ISO 27001, NIST, OWASP, SWC mapping github-pr CI/CD PR comment format

docs.title: LLM-Enhanced Reports
docs.text:
With --llm-interpret, reports include:

  • Executive Summary: model-assisted overview of security posture
  • Attack Scenarios: Realistic exploitation paths
  • Remediation Guidance: Detailed fix recommendations
  • Risk Assessment: Business impact analysis
# Premium report with all AI features
miesc report results.json \
  -t premium \
  --llm-interpret \
  --model mistral \
  -f pdf \
  -o premium-audit-report.pdf

docs.title: Output Formats
docs.text: Format Use Case Command PDF Client delivery -f pdf HTML Web viewing -f html Markdown Documentation -f markdown SARIF GitHub Code Scanning miesc export -f sarif CSV Spreadsheet analysis miesc export -f csv JSON Programmatic use Default output

docs.title: GitHub Actions
docs.text:
name: Security Audit

on:
  pull_request:
    paths:
      - 'contracts/**'

jobs:
  audit:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v6

      - name: Run MIESC Audit
        run: |
          docker run --rm \
            -v ${{ github.workspace }}:/contracts \
            ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:5.4.2 \
            audit quick /contracts \
            --ci \
            -o /contracts/results.json

      - name: Upload SARIF
        run: |
          docker run --rm \
            -v ${{ github.workspace }}:/contracts \
            ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:5.4.2 \
            export /contracts/results.json \
            -f sarif \
            -o /contracts/results.sarif.json

      - name: Upload to GitHub Security
        uses: github/codeql-action/upload-sarif@v4
        with:
          sarif_file: results.sarif.json

docs.title: Pre-commit Hook
docs.text:
# .pre-commit-config.yaml
repos:
  - repo: local
    hooks:
      - id: miesc-scan
        name: MIESC Security Scan
        entry: miesc scan
        language: system
        files: \.sol$
        args: ['--ci']

docs.title: GitLab CI
docs.text:
security-audit:
  image: ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:5.4.2
  stage: test
  script:
    - miesc audit quick ./contracts --ci -o results.json
  artifacts:
    paths:
      - results.json
    reports:
      sast: results.json

docs.title: Before the Audit
docs.text:
1. Understand the project scope
  2. Contract count and complexity
  3. Dependencies and external calls

  4. DeFi integrations (if any)

  5. Set up environment

  6. Use Docker for consistent results
  7. Ensure Ollama is running for LLM features

  8. Check tool availability with miesc doctor

  9. Review existing documentation

  10. Project README and specifications
  11. Previous audit reports (if any)
  12. Known issues or limitations

docs.title: During the Audit
docs.text:
1. Start with triage 

    miesc scan contract.sol

  2. Run quick audit first 

    miesc audit quick ./contracts -o results-quick.json

  3. Review critical findings immediately

  4. Don't wait for full audit to flag critical issues

  5. Communicate with client if blocking issues found

  6. Run full audit 

    miesc audit full ./contracts --skip-unavailable -o results-full.json

  7. Layer-specific deep dives

  8. Use audit layer for specific concerns

  9. Focus on layers relevant to contract type

  10. Document false positives

  11. Note why each false positive was dismissed
  12. Include in final report

docs.title: After the Audit
docs.text:
1. Generate multiple reports
  2. Premium/Professional for client
  3. Executive for management

  4. Technical for developers

  5. Verify remediation

  6. Re-run audit after fixes

  7. Use miesc benchmark to track improvement

  8. Archive results

  9. Keep JSON results for future reference
  10. Store reports with project documentation

docs.title: Workflow 1: Quick PR Review (15 minutes)
docs.text:
# 1. Quick scan
miesc scan ./contracts/NewFeature.sol

# 2. If issues found, get details
miesc audit quick ./contracts -o pr-review.json

# 3. Generate PR comment
miesc report pr-review.json -t github-pr -f markdown

docs.title: Workflow 2: Pre-deployment Audit (4 hours)
docs.text:
# 1. Triage
miesc scan ./contracts

# 2. Full audit
miesc audit full ./contracts --skip-unavailable -o full-audit.json

# 3. Generate reports
miesc report full-audit.json -t premium --llm-interpret -f pdf -o client-report.pdf
miesc report full-audit.json -t executive -f pdf -o executive-summary.pdf
miesc report full-audit.json -t technical -f markdown -o dev-findings.md

# 4. Export for CI
miesc export full-audit.json -f sarif -o results.sarif.json

docs.title: Workflow 3: DeFi Protocol Audit (1 day)
docs.text:
# 1. Quick triage
miesc scan ./contracts

# 2. Static analysis (Layer 1)
miesc audit layer 1 ./contracts -o layer1.json

# 3. Symbolic execution (Layer 3)
miesc audit layer 3 ./contracts -o layer3.json

# 4. Economic analysis (Layer 5)
miesc audit layer 5 ./contracts -o layer5.json

# 5. LLM analysis for DeFi patterns (Layer 7)
miesc audit layer 7 ./contracts -o layer7.json

# 6. Full audit for completeness
miesc audit full ./contracts --skip-unavailable -o full-audit.json

# 7. Premium report with AI
miesc report full-audit.json -t premium --llm-interpret -f pdf -o defi-audit.pdf

docs.title: Workflow 4: Continuous Monitoring
docs.text:
# 1. Set up baseline
miesc benchmark ./contracts --save

# 2. Watch for changes
miesc watch ./contracts --profile balanced

# 3. Compare after changes
miesc benchmark ./contracts --compare last

docs.title: Standard Image (Most Common)
docs.text:
# Basic audit
docker run --rm -v $(pwd):/contracts \
  ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:5.4.2 \
  audit quick /contracts -o /contracts/results.json

# With LLM (macOS)
docker run --rm -v $(pwd):/contracts \
  -e OLLAMA_HOST=http://host.docker.internal:11434 \
  ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:5.4.2 \
  audit full /contracts --skip-unavailable -o /contracts/results.json

docs.title: Full Image (Comprehensive)
docs.text:
# Full audit with all tools
docker run --rm -v $(pwd):/contracts \
  -e OLLAMA_HOST=http://host.docker.internal:11434 \
  ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:5.4.2-full \
  audit full /contracts --skip-unavailable -o /contracts/results.json

# Generate premium report
docker run --rm -v $(pwd):/contracts \
  -e OLLAMA_HOST=http://host.docker.internal:11434 \
  ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc:5.4.2-full \

  -t premium --llm-interpret -f pdf -o /contracts/report.pdf

docs.title: Support & Resources
docs.text:
• Documentation: https://github.com/fboiero/MIESC/tree/main/docs
  • Issues: https://github.com/fboiero/MIESC/issues
  • Docker Images: ghcr.io/fboiero/miesc
